title: Are most AI trading models underperforming simply because the inputs are too slow?
author: Training-Extent9606
selftext:
Been digging into this after noticing a pattern across a few systems.

A lot of models still rely heavily on:

OHLCV data, Derived indicators, Lagging volatility measures

Which worked fine when markets moved slower.

But right now, a lot of moves seem to be triggered by:

Sudden liquidity shifts, Positioning imbalances, Short term order flow changes

None of which are fully captured in standard datasets.

Started experimenting with:

Higher frequency data (where available)

Proxy signals for liquidity (wicks, failed breaks, rapid reversals)

Event driven filters instead of continuous signals

What’s interesting is:

Signal accuracy didn’t improve massively, but drawdowns became more controlled.

Feels like the edge isn’t better prediction, it’s avoiding bad conditions faster.

So now I’m questioning whether most “AI trading” underperformance is actually:

a modeling problem or just a data problem.

comments.author: Busy-Specialist7708
comments.body:
I think it is both, but the bigger issue is usually **state awareness**, not prediction quality.

A lot of AI trading models are trained to ask:

“Where does price go next?”

But the better question is:

“Is this environment even safe to take the signal?”

That is where standard OHLCV models struggle. They may detect pattern, momentum, volatility, trend, etc., but they often miss the thing that actually kills the trade: liquidity shift, event shock, positioning unwind, regime change, or forced flow.

So yes, the inputs can be too slow. But even with faster inputs, the model still needs a hierarchy.

For me the useful distinction is:

**Slow layer:** defines the broader regime  
**Fast layer:** detects event stress, liquidity breaks, failed transmission, forced positioning  
**Execution layer:** only acts if the first two layers allow it

That is why your point about drawdowns is important. Faster data may not make the model “smarter” at predicting direction, but it can make it better at knowing when not to participate.

And that is probably where a lot of real edge sits.

Not “AI predicts better.”  
More like: **AI exits bad environments faster than a human ego does.**

Most models underperform because they treat every signal as equal across every environment. But markets are not equal across environments. A breakout during stable flow and a breakout during event noise are not the same trade.

So I would say:

Data problem, yes.  
Modeling problem, yes.  
But mainly a **regime/context problem**.

The model needs to know when its own signal quality is degrading.

replies.author: Training-Extent9606
replies.body:
This is a solid way to frame it, especially the idea of signal quality degrading across regimes.

What I’ve found is that most systems don’t fail on direction, they fail on *permission*. They keep firing in conditions where they probably shouldn’t be active at all.

I’ve been experimenting with tools like GetClaw recently, and what stood out wasn’t prediction, but how it tries to map flow and stress in real time. Not perfect, but it leans more into that “should I even be here?” layer you’re describing.

Feels like that layer is where a lot of the edge is shifting lately.

comments.author: hydronos
comments.body:
The “permission” layer is honestly the biggest thing we found too.

A lot of systems are decent at finding directional setups. The real damage comes from taking them in terrible environments.

We started seeing way better stability once we stopped treating every signal equally and started filtering harder based on regime, liquidity, IV conditions, failed continuation attempts, chop vs trend days etc.

Funny enough the raw directional accuracy barely changed. The drawdowns did.

That’s what shifted my thinking away from “predict better” toward “participate smarter.”

replies.author: Training-Extent9606
replies.body: That’s a solid shift. Most people obsess over signal quality, but it’s really a selection problem. A mediocre signal in the right regime outperforms a great one in the wrong environment almost every time.

comments.author: AffectionateDrop2155
comments.body:
i think a lot of it is trying to do more than just 'one' thing. whether it be incorporating options and greeks, going both long and short in a single strategy. we're trying to break into the quant world and they have SO much more money to build this shit than we do, even if the playing field has been equalized somewhat 

you can get fairly high frequency data off ninjatrader if you're willing to sit there and let it download, as well as solid market replay. which i prefer to backtesting across almost all platforms. every time i had a positive backtest, a problem would come up in reality.

comments.author: misscoffeeaddict2
comments.body: You nailed it with the drawdown point. The real edge right now is just staying out of toxic market conditions rather than trying to predict the exact top or bottom. I use signalwhisper precisely because it filters for those shifts in real-time sentiment and order flow, which helps keep the drawdowns a lot tighter.

comments.author: Fun-Society-1763
comments.body: Latency is a factor, but data quality and structure are often bigger bottlenecks. Models fed with noisy data will always underperform. Platforms like QuantPlace focus heavily on providing pre-processed, AI-ready datasets to ensure your models are trained on the cleanest inputs possible
